title: CIL System — Advanced Mock 11 (Conceptual)
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: As per Income Tax Department data (June 2026), India's net direct tax collections in early FY27 rose by about what percentage year-on-year?
questions.solution: Net direct tax collections rose ~14.6% YoY to about Rs 5.21 trillion.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, which country became the first Central Asian member of the New Development Bank (NDB)?
questions.solution: Uzbekistan became the first Central Asian nation to join the BRICS-backed NDB.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which India-born theoretical physicist won the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics?
questions.solution: Rajasthan-born physicist Jainendra K. Jain received the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which institution ranked first in the QS World University Rankings 2027 (released June 2026)?
questions.solution: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) ranked first in QS World University Rankings 2027.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In June 2026, which Indian jurist was elected a Judge of the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)?
questions.solution: Indian jurist Bimal N. Patel was elected to ITLOS in June 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, PM Narendra Modi received the highest national award of which country?
questions.solution: PM Modi was conferred Slovakia's highest national award in 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026, in which an Indian Army contingent participated, was held in which country?
questions.solution: The multinational Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026 was held in Mongolia.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was named the new Chairman of GIC Re in June 2026?
questions.solution: The government named Hitesh Joshi as the new Chairman of GIC Re.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, ONGC was assigned to develop a new strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) facility at which location?
questions.solution: ONGC was tasked with building a new underground crude oil SPR cavern at Mangaluru, Karnataka.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The India–UK CETA and Social Security Agreement were reported to take effect from which date?
questions.solution: Reports indicated the India–UK CETA would take effect from July 15, 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the chemical symbol of Gold?
questions.solution: Gold's symbol is Au (from Latin 'aurum'); Ag is silver.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the chemical symbol of Sodium?
questions.solution: Sodium's symbol is Na (from Latin 'natrium'); Sn is tin.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The most abundant metal in the Earth's crust is:
questions.solution: Aluminium is the most abundant metal in the Earth's crust.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who chaired the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was the first President of India?
questions.solution: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India (1950–62).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: How many Fundamental Rights are currently guaranteed by the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution: There are six Fundamental Rights after the Right to Property was removed in 1978.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Directive Principles of State Policy are enshrined in which Part of the Constitution?
questions.solution: Directive Principles are contained in Part IV (Articles 36–51).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who is the constitutional head of the Indian Union?
questions.solution: The President of India is the constitutional head of the Union.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national animal of India?
questions.solution: The Royal Bengal Tiger is the national animal of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national bird of India?
questions.solution: The Indian Peafowl (peacock) is the national bird of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national flower of India?
questions.solution: The lotus is the national flower of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the longest river in India?
questions.solution: The Ganga is the longest river flowing within India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the highest mountain peak entirely within India?
questions.solution: Kangchenjunga is the highest peak located within India's borders.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Tropic of Cancer passes through how many Indian states?
questions.solution: The Tropic of Cancer passes through eight Indian states.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the currency of Japan?
questions.solution: The Yen is the currency of Japan; Yuan is Chinese, Won is Korean.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A invests Rs 4000 for 12 months and B invests Rs 6000 for 4 months. Out of a total profit of Rs 6000, A's share is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit is split in the ratio of capital × time invested by each partner.
**Formula.** $\text{share}_A=P\cdot\tfrac{x\,m_A}{x\,m_A+y\,m_B}$
**Given.** A: Rs 4000×12 mo; B: Rs 6000×4 mo; profit Rs 6000.
**Working.** Ratio $=48000:24000$; share$_A=6000\times\tfrac{48000}{72000}$.
**Answer.** A's share $=$ Rs 4000.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A boat covers 60 km downstream in 6 h and the same 60 km upstream in 10 h. Its speed in still water is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Downstream speed adds the stream; upstream subtracts it. Still-water speed is the average of the two.
**Formula.** $u=\tfrac12\left[(b+s)+(b-s)\right]$
**Given.** Downstream 60 km in 6 h; upstream 60 km in 10 h.
**Working.** Downstream $=\tfrac{60}{6}=10$ km/h, upstream $=\tfrac{60}{10}=6$ km/h.
**Answer.** Still-water speed $u=\tfrac{10+6}{2}=8$ km/h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A trader marks goods 40% above cost, then gives a 25% discount. His net profit percentage is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net selling factor is the markup factor times the discount factor; profit% is that factor reduced by 1.
**Formula.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{m}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{d}{100}\right)$
**Given.** Markup m = 40%, discount d = 25%.
**Working.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{40}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{25}{100}\right)=1.05$.
**Answer.** Profit $=(f-1)\times100=5\%$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Two trains 180 m and 200 m long move in opposite directions at 72 km/h and 48 km/h. The time to cross each other is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Opposite directions add speeds; convert km/h to m/s with the factor 5/18, then divide the combined length by the relative speed.
**Formula.** $t=\tfrac{L_1+L_2}{(s_1+s_2)\times\frac{5}{18}}$
**Given.** L₁ = 180 m, L₂ = 200 m, s₁ = 72 km/h, s₂ = 48 km/h.
**Working.** Relative $=(72+48)\times\tfrac{5}{18}=33.33$ m/s; $t=\tfrac{180+200}{33.33}$.
**Answer.** $t=11.4$ s.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A pipe fills a tank in 8 h, but with a leak it takes 9 h. The leak alone can empty the full tank in:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The leak's rate is the fill-rate without the leak minus the slower fill-rate with the leak.
**Formula.** $T=\tfrac{ab}{b-a}$
**Given.** Fill without leak a = 8 h; with leak b = 9 h.
**Working.** Leak rate $=\tfrac1{8}-\tfrac1{9}$; emptying time $=\tfrac{8\times9}{9-8}$.
**Answer.** $T=72$ h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A vessel holds 100 L of milk. 10 L is drawn out and replaced with water; this is repeated 2 times. The milk remaining is about:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each draw-and-replace keeps the fraction $(1-x/V)$ of milk; after n repeats this fraction is raised to the n-th power.
**Formula.** $M=V\left(1-\tfrac{x}{V}\right)^{n}$
**Given.** V = 100 L, x = 10 L, n = 2.
**Working.** $=100\left(1-\tfrac{10}{100}\right)^{2}$.
**Answer.** $M\approx 81$ L.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The difference between compound and simple interest on Rs 8000 at 5% per annum for 2 years is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Over 2 years the gap between compound and simple interest is the first year's interest-on-interest.
**Formula.** $CI-SI=P\left(\tfrac{r}{100}\right)^2$
**Given.** P = Rs 8000, r = 5% p.a., t = 2 yr.
**Working.** $=8000\left(\tfrac{5}{100}\right)^2$.
**Answer.** $CI-SI=$ Rs 20.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The angle between the hour and minute hands of a clock at 4:36 is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The hour hand sweeps 30° per hour and 0.5° per minute, the minute hand 6° per minute; take the smaller of the two angles.
**Formula.** $\theta=\left|30H-5.5M\right|$
**Given.** H = 4, M = 36.
**Working.** $=\left|30\times4-5.5\times36\right|=78^\circ$; smaller $=\min(78,360-78)$.
**Answer.** $\theta=78^\circ$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A invests Rs 5000 for 6 months and B invests Rs 6000 for 6 months. Out of a total profit of Rs 6000, A's share is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit is split in the ratio of capital × time invested by each partner.
**Formula.** $\text{share}_A=P\cdot\tfrac{x\,m_A}{x\,m_A+y\,m_B}$
**Given.** A: Rs 5000×6 mo; B: Rs 6000×6 mo; profit Rs 6000.
**Working.** Ratio $=30000:36000$; share$_A=6000\times\tfrac{30000}{66000}$.
**Answer.** A's share $=$ Rs 2727.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The LCM of 9 and 12 is:
questions.solution: The lowest common multiple of 9 and 12 is 36.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The average age of 5 persons is 30 years. If a person aged 38 leaves, the average of the remaining 4 is:
questions.solution: Total = 150; remaining = 150 − 38 = 112; average = 28.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: If 20% of a number is 60, the number is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The compound ratio of 2 : 3 and 6 : 7 is:
questions.solution: Compound ratio = (2×6) : (3×7) = 12 : 21.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The percentage increase when a value rises from 60 to 75 is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A sum doubles itself in 5 years under simple interest. The annual rate is:
questions.solution: To double, SI = P, so rate = 100/T = 100/5 = 20%.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A and B invest in the ratio 5 : 5. Out of a total profit of Rs 12000, A's share is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The probability of getting an even number on a single throw of a fair die is:
questions.solution: Count the favourable outcomes out of 6 equally likely faces to get 1/2.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Compound interest on Rs 10000 at 8% per annum for 2 years is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An item bought for Rs 1200 is sold for Rs 1344. The profit percentage is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A can do a work in 12 days and B in 24 days. Working together they finish it in:
questions.solution: Combined rate = 1/12 + 1/24; time = 12×24/(12+24) = 8 days.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Successive discounts of 15% and 10% on a marked price of Rs 2000 give a final price of:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An article sold at Rs 400 incurs a 20% loss. Its cost price is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: 2 kg of rice at Rs 40/kg is mixed with 6 kg at Rs 80/kg. The average price per kg is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The HCF of 36 and 48 is:
questions.solution: The highest common factor of 36 and 48 is 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Simple interest on Rs 5000 at 7% per annum for 4 years is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 9 km towards north, then turns east and walks 12 km. How far is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: Straight-line distance = √(9² + 12²) = √225 = 15 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term in the series: 2, 5, 11, 23, 47, ?
questions.solution: Each term = previous × 2 + 1, so the next term = 47 × 2 + 1 = 95.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All pens are books. All books are tables. Which conclusion necessarily follows?
questions.solution: Applying the rules of categorical syllogism, the valid conclusion is: All pens are tables.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A = 1, B = 2, … , Z = 26, what is the sum of the letter values of 'BID'?
questions.solution: Add the positional values of B, I, D to get 15.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a certain code, each letter of a word is moved two steps forward in the English alphabet. The code for 'LAMP' is:
questions.solution: Replace every letter by the one two places ahead: LAMP → NCOR.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: X is the husband of Y. Z is the mother of Y. How is Z related to X?
questions.solution: Tracing each relationship step by step gives: Mother-in-law.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 5 km towards north, then turns east and walks 12 km. How far is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: Straight-line distance = √(5² + 12²) = √169 = 13 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term in the series: 5, 11, 23, 47, 95, ?
questions.solution: Each term = previous × 2 + 1, so the next term = 95 × 2 + 1 = 191.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 16, 25, 36, 49, ?
questions.solution: These are perfect squares; next = 8² = 64.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: All pens are tools. All tools are useful. Which conclusion follows?
questions.solution: Both premises are universal affirmatives, so the chained conclusion 'All pens are useful' follows.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A person facing east turns 90° to the right. Now they face:
questions.solution: Turning right from east leads to South.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If 3 January is a Wednesday, what day is 10 January?
questions.solution: 10 − 3 = 7 days, exactly one week, so it is again Wednesday.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If each letter is replaced by the next letter, TREE is coded as:
questions.solution: Shift each letter forward by one to get USFF.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.solution: Each number is halved, so 16 → 8.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Pointing to a man, a woman said, 'His mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man?
questions.solution: The only daughter of her mother is the woman herself, so she is the man's mother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: 1, 8, 20, 27?
questions.solution: The others are perfect cubes; 20 is not.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which of these fractions is the largest?
questions.solution: Comparing decimal values, 4/5 is the largest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 2, 6, 18, 54, ?
questions.solution: Each term is multiplied by 3, so next = 54 × 3 = 162.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A=1, B=2, ... then the sum of the letters of BED is:
questions.solution: Add the alphabet positions of B, E, D to get 11.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Hand : Glove :: Foot : ?
questions.solution: Just as Glove relates to Hand, Sock relates to Foot.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: Z, X, V, T, ?
questions.solution: Letters decrease by 2 each time, so the next letter is R.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row of 20 people, X is 9th from the left. X's position from the right is:
questions.solution: Position from right = 20 − 9 + 1 = 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: DE, GH, JK, ?
questions.solution: Each pair skips one letter; after JK comes MN.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: Apple, Mango, Banana, Potato?
questions.solution: Potato belongs to a different category from the rest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He is married ___ a doctor.'
questions.solution: The correct collocation is 'married to' someone.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'The book is ___ the table.'
questions.solution: 'On' indicates resting on a surface.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One word for 'a person who cannot read or write':
questions.solution: Such a person is 'illiterate'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The study of living organisms is called:
questions.solution: 'Biology' is the study of all living organisms.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One who studies stars and celestial bodies is an:
questions.solution: An 'astronomer' scientifically studies celestial bodies.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One word for 'a speech made without preparation':
questions.solution: An unprepared speech is delivered 'extempore'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: A place where birds are kept is called:
questions.solution: An 'aviary' houses birds; an apiary keeps bees.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to break the ice' means:
questions.solution: 'Break the ice' means to initiate conversation in a tense or new setting.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'a piece of cake' means:
questions.solution: 'A piece of cake' means something very easy to do.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'once in a blue moon' means:
questions.solution: 'Once in a blue moon' means something happening very rarely.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to bury the hatchet' means:
questions.solution: 'Bury the hatchet' means to end a quarrel and make peace.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to let the cat out of the bag' means:
questions.solution: It means to accidentally reveal a secret.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Child' is:
questions.solution: 'Child' has the irregular plural 'children'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Mouse' is:
questions.solution: 'Mouse' has the irregular plural 'mice'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Neither he nor I ___ wrong.'
questions.solution: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the nearer subject 'I', so 'am'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Each of the students ___ a book.'
questions.solution: 'Each' is singular, so it takes 'has'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He is ___ honest man.'
questions.solution: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound, so 'an' is used.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'She plays ___ violin beautifully.'
questions.solution: Musical instruments take the definite article 'the'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'John wrote the letter.' is:
options: The letter is written by John
The letter was written by John
questions.solution: Past simple active becomes 'was written' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'She is reading a book.' is:
options: A book was read by her
A book has been read by her
A book is read by her
A book is being read by her
questions.solution: Present continuous active becomes 'is being read' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'He / don't / like / coffee.'
questions.solution: Third-person singular needs 'doesn't', not 'don't'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'One of the boys / are / playing / outside.'
questions.solution: 'One of the boys' is singular, so it requires 'is'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'I ___ my homework already.'
questions.solution: 'Already' signals present perfect, so 'have done' is correct.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'By next year, she ___ here for a decade.'
questions.solution: A completed action by a future point uses the future perfect 'will have worked'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the word that means the opposite of 'Artificial'.
questions.solution: 'Artificial' is opposite to 'natural'.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pointer variable stores:
options: The memory address of another variable
questions.solution: Pointers hold addresses, enabling indirect access and dynamic structures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The average-case time to search a well-distributed hash table is:
questions.solution: With a good hash function and low load factor, lookup is expected constant time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 2K memory locations?
questions.solution: 2K = 2048 = 2^11, so 11 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A switch (LAN) forwards frames using:
options: MAC addresses at the data-link layer
Port numbers at the transport layer
IP addresses at the network layer
questions.solution: A Layer-2 switch learns and forwards by MAC address.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(4) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 5, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Encapsulation in object-oriented programming refers to:
options: Bundling data with the methods that operate on it
questions.solution: Encapsulation groups state and behaviour and restricts direct access to internals.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a 5-stage instruction pipeline, a data hazard arises when:
options: An instruction needs a result not yet produced by an earlier instruction
questions.solution: Data hazards occur from dependencies; they are mitigated by forwarding or stalls.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 4-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DNS is responsible for:
options: Translating domain names to IP addresses
questions.solution: The Domain Name System resolves human-readable names to IP addresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NAND gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 0, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The base (radix) of the hexadecimal number system is:
questions.solution: Hexadecimal uses 16 symbols (0–9, A–F).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of n elements runs in:
questions.solution: Each comparison halves the search space, giving logarithmic time — but the array must be sorted.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 33 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A problem that is NP-complete is:
options: Solvable in polynomial time for sure
In NP and at least as hard as every NP problem
questions.solution: NP-complete = in NP and NP-hard; a polynomial solution to one would solve all of NP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number FF₁₆ is:
questions.solution: FF₁₆ = 255₁₀.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TCP connection is established using a:
questions.solution: TCP setup uses SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK; teardown uses a four-way exchange.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a direct-mapped cache, each main-memory block can be placed in:
questions.solution: Direct mapping fixes each block to one line (block mod #lines), unlike associative caches.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 10 ns, memory access = 100 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.9 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: B-trees are preferred over binary search trees for on-disk databases because they:
options: Are shallow (high fan-out), reducing disk accesses
questions.solution: High branching factor keeps B-trees shallow, minimizing expensive disk seeks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 100 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 10 ms. The effective access time is about:
questions.solution: EAT = (1−p)·ma + p·service = 1×100 + 0.002×10ms ≈ 20099.8 ns (faults dominate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A semaphore is primarily used for:
questions.solution: Semaphores coordinate access to shared resources, preventing race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an n-bit two's-complement representation, the number of distinct values is:
questions.solution: n bits encode 2ⁿ distinct values, from −2ⁿ⁻¹ to 2ⁿ⁻¹ − 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 10 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.9. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: UDP, in contrast to TCP, is:
questions.solution: UDP sends datagrams without setup, acknowledgements or guaranteed delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 5 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The portion of code where a process accesses shared resources is the:
questions.solution: Mutual exclusion must be enforced over the critical section to avoid race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting n elements into a dynamic array that doubles on resize has an amortized insertion cost of:
questions.solution: Doubling makes total work O(n) over n inserts, i.e. amortized O(1) each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Borrowing 4 bits from the host portion of a class-C network yields how many subnets, each with how many usable hosts?
questions.solution: Subnets = 2^4 = 16; host bits = 4 ⇒ 2^4 − 2 = 14 hosts each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL clause used to retrieve data from a table is:
questions.solution: SELECT … FROM … queries rows from one or more tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the binary number 1111₂ is:
questions.solution: 1111₂ = 15₁₀ (sum of place values).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Von Neumann architecture, instructions and data:
questions.solution: Von Neumann machines store programs and data in one common memory (Harvard uses separate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization of a database is performed mainly to:
options: Reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies
questions.solution: Normalization organizes data to minimize duplication and anomalies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the OR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">OR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: OR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A primary key in a relation must be:
questions.solution: A primary key uniquely identifies each tuple and cannot be null.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 10 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory allows a program to:
options: Use more address space than physical RAM
questions.solution: Virtual memory backs part of the address space on disk, giving the illusion of large RAM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Demand paging brings a page into memory:
options: At program load time, all at once
Only when it is first referenced (causing a page fault)
questions.solution: Pages are loaded lazily on first access; the page fault triggers the load.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 100 Mbps has a round-trip time of 50 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:
questions.solution: BDP = bandwidth×delay = 100Mbps×50ms ÷ 8 ≈ 610.35 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The NAND and NOR gates are called universal gates because they can:
questions.solution: Any logic function can be built using only NAND (or only NOR) gates.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number A₁₆ is:
questions.solution: A₁₆ = 10₁₀.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about virtual memory is INCORRECT?
options: The page table maps pages to frames
Virtual memory can exceed physical RAM
Page faults are handled entirely by the CPU without disk access
A page fault may load a page from disk
questions.solution: A page fault for a non-resident page requires the OS to fetch it from disk.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thrashing in a virtual-memory system refers to:
options: Excessive paging that cripples useful work
questions.solution: When processes spend more time swapping pages than computing, the system thrashes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 40), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 7, 15, 20, 40, 65.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">40</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">15</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">65</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">20</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which sorting algorithm is in-place (O(1) extra space)?
questions.solution: Quicksort sorts within the array; standard merge sort needs O(n) auxiliary space.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of 1024 elements needs at most how many comparisons?
questions.solution: Worst-case comparisons ≈ ⌈log₂ 1024⌉ = 10.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement comparing compilation and interpretation is correct?
options: Compiled code generally runs faster than interpreted code
questions.solution: Ahead-of-time compiled native code typically executes faster than line-by-line interpretation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A router operates principally at the OSI:
questions.solution: Routers forward packets using IP addresses at the network layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 9, push 12, pop, push 30. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 30 leaves 30 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">30</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache memory is used because it is:
options: Faster than main memory, reducing access time
questions.solution: Cache is small, fast memory holding frequently used data near the CPU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 12, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about greedy algorithms is correct?
options: They do not always yield a globally optimal solution
They are the same as dynamic programming
questions.solution: Greedy choices are optimal only for problems with the greedy-choice property (e.g. MST); not in general.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: Elements leave a queue in the same order they entered — FIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the XOR gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">XOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: XOR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which logic gate outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1?
questions.solution: AND gives 1 only when every input is 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.8. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Given the network 192.168.1.0/26, the number of usable host addresses per subnet is:
questions.solution: /26 leaves 6 host bits ⇒ 2⁶ − 2 = 62 usable hosts (minus network and broadcast).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A compiler differs from an interpreter in that it:
options: Translates the whole program before execution
Executes line by line at run time
questions.solution: A compiler produces object/machine code ahead of time; an interpreter executes statements directly.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /29 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?
questions.solution: Host bits = 32 − 29 = 3; usable = 2^3 − 2 = 6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polymorphism in OOP allows:
options: One interface to take many forms
questions.solution: Polymorphism lets the same call behave differently depending on the object's type.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 5 elements is:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes n(n−1)/2 = 5×4/2 = 10 comparisons.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For every nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA) there exists:
questions.solution: NFAs and DFAs recognize exactly the same (regular) languages — the subset construction proves it.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Quicksort attains its O(n²) worst case when:
options: The pivot is always the median
The pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element
The array is large but random
questions.solution: Maximally unbalanced partitions (e.g. sorted input with first/last pivot) give O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 4 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Linear search of an unsorted array of n items takes, in the worst case:
questions.solution: It may examine every element, so worst-case time is O(n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 24 bits occupies how many bytes?
questions.solution: 24 bits ÷ 8 = 3 bytes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which comparison of sorting algorithms is correct?
options: Merge sort is stable; standard quicksort is not
Quicksort is stable; merge sort is not
questions.solution: Merge sort preserves equal-key order (stable); typical quicksort does not.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Searching a skewed (degenerate) binary search tree of n nodes takes, in the worst case:
questions.solution: A fully skewed BST behaves like a linked list, giving O(n) search.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 60), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 32, 40, 45, 60, 85.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">60</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">40</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">85</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">32</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">45</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about a singly linked list is correct?
options: Its size must be fixed at creation
It does not support O(1) random access by index
questions.solution: Linked lists require O(n) traversal to reach an arbitrary index — no random access.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 200 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 10 ms. The effective access time is about:
questions.solution: EAT = (1−p)·ma + p·service = 1×200 + 0.002×10ms ≈ 20199.6 ns (faults dominate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: To find shortest paths when some edge weights are negative (but no negative cycle), use:
questions.solution: Bellman–Ford handles negative edges and detects negative cycles; Dijkstra cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TCP provides a service that is:
questions.solution: TCP uses handshakes, acknowledgements and retransmission for reliable, ordered delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interpreted as an 8-bit two's-complement number, the bit pattern 11111010 equals:
questions.solution: MSB = 1 ⇒ negative; value = 250 − 256 = -6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAM is described as volatile memory because it:
options: Loses its contents when power is removed
questions.solution: Volatile memory needs power to keep data; RAM clears on power-off.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hash table with 100 slots holds 30 keys. Its load factor is:
questions.solution: Load factor α = n/m = 30/100 = 0.3.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The adjacency-matrix representation of a graph with V vertices needs space:
questions.solution: A V×V matrix stores every possible edge, using O(V²) space regardless of edge count.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A max-heap is a complete binary tree in which each parent is:
options: Greater than or equal to its children
questions.solution: In a max-heap every parent ≥ its children, putting the maximum at the root.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 8-bit register can represent how many distinct values?
questions.solution: 8 bits give 2^8 = 256 distinct patterns.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In 8-bit two's complement, the range of representable integers is:
questions.solution: 8-bit two's complement spans −2⁷ to 2⁷ − 1 = −128 to +127.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 50×80 array of 4-byte elements occupies how much memory?
questions.solution: Size = rows×cols×bytes = 50×80×4 = 16000 B = 15.63 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting a node at the front of a singly linked list takes time:
questions.solution: Only a couple of pointer updates are needed — constant time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is INCORRECT?
options: Linear search works on unsorted arrays
Binary search requires a sorted array
Binary search works on an unsorted array
Binary search runs in O(log n)
questions.solution: Binary search only works on sorted data — the claim that it works unsorted is false.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The truth table shown (output Y) corresponds to which logic function of A and B?
questions.solution: Reading the output column identifies the function as OR.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 170 150" width="170" height="150"><text x="26" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">A</text><text x="66" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">B</text><text x="112" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">Y</text><line x1="20" y1="40" x2="150" y2="40" stroke="#475569"/><text x="30" y="55" font-size="11">0</text><text x="70" y="55" font-size="11">0</text><text x="120" y="55" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">0</text><text x="30" y="75" font-size="11">0</text><text x="70" y="75" font-size="11">1</text><text x="120" y="75" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">1</text><text x="30" y="95" font-size="11">1</text><text x="70" y="95" font-size="11">0</text><text x="120" y="95" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">1</text><text x="30" y="115" font-size="11">1</text><text x="70" y="115" font-size="11">1</text><text x="120" y="115" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">1</text></svg>
figure.caption: Truth table

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key enforces:
questions.solution: A foreign key must match a primary key in the referenced table (or be null), enforcing referential integrity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the graph shown (edges A–B, A–C, B–D, C–D), a breadth-first search from A, visiting neighbours alphabetically, gives the order:
questions.solution: BFS from A visits A, then its neighbours B and C, then D: A, B, C, D.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 140" width="220" height="140"><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="160" y2="30" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="40" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="160" y1="30" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="110" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="40" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="40" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">A</text><circle cx="160" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="160" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">B</text><circle cx="40" cy="110" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="40" y="114" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">C</text><circle cx="160" cy="110" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="160" y="114" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">D</text></svg>
figure.caption: Undirected graph (4 nodes)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The well-known TCP port for unencrypted HTTP is:
questions.solution: HTTP uses port 80; HTTPS uses 443.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A stack follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: The most recently pushed element is the first to be popped — LIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 200×40 array of 8-byte elements occupies how much memory?
questions.solution: Size = rows×cols×bytes = 200×40×8 = 64000 B = 62.5 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In round-robin CPU scheduling, each process is given:
options: A fixed time quantum in turn
questions.solution: Round-robin cycles through ready processes, each running for one time quantum.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 2 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 2KB = 2^11 bytes, so the offset field is 11 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An index on a database column generally:
options: Speeds up reads but can slow down writes
Speeds up both reads and writes equally
questions.solution: Indexes accelerate lookups but add overhead to inserts/updates that must maintain them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The OSI reference model has how many layers?
questions.solution: OSI: Physical, Data-link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NAND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NAND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: NAND gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ACID properties of a transaction stand for:
questions.solution: ACID guarantees reliable transaction processing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 100/250 = 0.4 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A flip-flop is a circuit that can store:
questions.solution: A flip-flop is a basic 1-bit memory element.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An in-order traversal of a binary search tree visits the keys in:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST yields keys in ascending order.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 3, push 11, push 14, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">11</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">3</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Accessing the i-th element of an array by index takes time:
questions.solution: Arrays support constant-time random access via base address + index×size.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 50), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 30, 35, 40, 50, 70.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">50</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">35</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">70</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">30</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">40</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree
